content: Imagine a system of unit in which the unit of mass is 10 kg , length is 1 km and time is 1 min. Then, 1 J in this system is equal to
explanation:
Imagine a system of units where the unit of mass is 10 kg, the unit of length is 1 km, and the unit of time is 1 minute. We need to determine the equivalent of 1 joule in this system.

The joule (J) is the SI unit of energy with a dimensional formula of $[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{T}^{-2}]$.

To convert one unit into another, we'll use the relationship:

$ \eta_1\left[M_1 L_1^2 T_1^{-2}\right] = \eta_2\left[M_2 L_2^2 T_2^{-2}\right] $

Solving for $\eta_2$:

$ \eta_2 = \eta_1 \left[\frac{M_1}{M_2}\right]^1 \left[\frac{L_1}{L_2}\right]^2 \left[\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right]^{-2} $

Substituting the given values:

$ \begin{aligned} & \eta_2 = 1 \times \left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~kg}}{10 \mathrm{~kg}}\right]^1 \left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~m}}{1 \mathrm{~km}}\right]^2 \left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~s}}{1 \mathrm{~min}}\right]^{-2} \\ & = 1 \times \left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~kg}}{10 \mathrm{~kg}}\right]^1 \left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~m}}{10^3 \mathrm{~m}}\right]^2 \left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~s}}{60 \mathrm{~s}}\right]^{-2} \\ & = 1 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-6} \times 36 \times 10^2 \end{aligned} $

Thus, the conversion yields:

$ \eta_2 = 36 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~J} $

content: In the formula $$X=3 Y Z^2, X$$ and $$Z$$ have dimensions of capacitance and magnetic induction respectively. The dimensions of $$Y$$ is MKS system are.
explanation:
$$\text { Dimensions of } Y=\frac{\text { Dimensions of } X}{\text { Dimensions of } Z^2}$$

$$=\frac{\left[M^{-1} L^{-2} T^4 A^2\right]}{\left[M T^{-2} A^{-1}\right]^2}=\left[M^{-3} L^{-2} T^8 A^4\right]$$

content: A quantity $X$ is given by $$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{L} \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta t}$$, where $$\varepsilon_0$$ is the permittivity of free space, $$L$$ is the length, $$A$$ is area, $$\Delta V$$ is the potential difference and $$\Delta t$$ is time interval. The dimensional formula for $$X$$ is the same as that of
explanation:
To find the dimensional formula for the quantity $X$, we first write down the dimensional formulas for each term in the equation for $X = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{L} \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta t}$.

Here are the dimensional formulas for all the components involved:

  • $ [ \varepsilon_0 ] = M^{-1} L^{-3} T^{4} I^{2} $ (Permittivity of free space)

  • $ [ A ] = L^{2} $ (Area)

  • $ [ L ] = L $ (Length)

  • $ [\Delta V] = M L^{2} T^{-3} I^{-1} $ (Potential difference / Voltage)

  • $ [ \Delta t ] = T $ (Time)

We can now substitute these into the equation to find the dimensional formula for $X$.

$ [ X ] = [ \varepsilon_0 ] \cdot \frac{[ A ]}{[ L ]} \cdot \frac{[\Delta V]}{[ \Delta t ]}$

This simplifies to:

$ [ X ] = M^{-1} L^{-3} T^{4} I^{2} \cdot \frac{L^{2}}{L} \cdot \frac{M L^{2} T^{-3} I^{-1}}{T}$

Reducing this formula leads to:

$ [ X ] = M^{-1} L^{-3} T^{4} I^{2} \cdot L^{1} \cdot M L^{2} T^{-4} I^{-1} = M^{0} L^{0} T^{0} I^{1}$

So, $ [ X ] = I $, meaning the dimensional formula of $X$ corresponds to current (Amperes).

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: current.
